topic: Revolt of 1857 — The First War of Independence

questions.text: The Revolt of 1857 formally began with the sepoy mutiny at which location, and on what date?
options: Barrackpore — 29 March 1857
Meerut — 10 May 1857
Delhi — 11 May 1857
Kanpur — 5 June 1857
questions.explanation: The Revolt of 1857 formally began with the sepoy mutiny at Meerut on 10 May 1857, when soldiers of the 3rd Cavalry broke open the jail, freed 85 colleagues who had been sentenced to hard labour for refusing to use the greased Enfield cartridges, killed British officers, and marched to Delhi. The events at Barrackpore (29 March) — where Mangal Pandey attacked British officers — preceded this but were an individual act, not a coordinated revolt. Delhi was reached on 11 May (the day after), when Bahadur Shah Zafar was proclaimed Emperor. The date 10 May 1857, Meerut, is the universally tested starting point of the 1857 Revolt.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the starting date (10 May 1857) and location (Meerut) of the 1857 revolt is one of the most consistently tested dates in Modern History

questions.text: Who coined the term 'First War of Independence' for the events of 1857?
options: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
Jawaharlal Nehru
Vinayak Damodar Savarkar
Bipin Chandra Pal
questions.explanation: Vinayak Damodar Savarkar (V.D. Savarkar) coined the term 'First War of Independence' in his 1909 book 'The Indian War of Independence 1857' — written in Marathi and translated to English. The book argued that 1857 was not a disorganised mutiny but a planned national uprising representing Indian consciousness against British colonialism. The British banned the book before it was even published in India (it was published in London). Savarkar's framing was politically motivated — he wanted to inspire the emerging nationalist movement. Modern historians have debated this: R.C. Majumdar argued it was not a national war (different groups had different grievances); S.N. Sen took a middle view.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the 'First War of Independence' terminology and Savarkar is a standard attribution question

questions.text: Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi died fighting the British. Where did she die, and in which month and year?
options: At Jhansi Fort — March 1858
At Gwalior — June 1858
At Lucknow — November 1857
At Kanpur — July 1857
questions.explanation: Rani Lakshmibai did NOT die at Jhansi — this is a very common exam trap. Jhansi fell to General Hugh Rose in April 1858. Lakshmibai escaped on horseback (some accounts say her horse jumped the fort walls) and reached Gwalior, where she joined Tatya Tope's forces. She died in battle at Gwalior in June 1858. General Hugh Rose described her as 'the best and bravest military leader of the rebels.' Her state of Jhansi had been annexed under the Doctrine of Lapse because her adopted son was not recognised by Dalhousie. Her famous declaration was 'Main apni Jhansi nahi doongi' (I will not give up my Jhansi).
questions.source: SSC CGL — Rani Lakshmibai's death at Gwalior (not Jhansi) in June 1858 is one of the most tested 'location of death' facts in 1857 questions

questions.text: What happened to Bahadur Shah Zafar II after the fall of Delhi in September 1857?
options: He was publicly executed at the Red Fort along with his sons
He was captured at Humayun's Tomb, tried, and exiled to Rangoon (Burma), where he died in 1862
He escaped to Nepal and lived there in exile until his natural death
He was pardoned by Lord Canning and allowed to remain in Delhi under house arrest
questions.explanation: Bahadur Shah Zafar II was captured at Humayun's Tomb in Delhi (where he had taken shelter) by British officer William Hodson. Before bringing Zafar to Delhi, Hodson shot and killed Zafar's two sons — Mirza Mughal and Mirza Khizr Sultan — and a grandson at Humayun's Tomb, claiming he feared rescue attempts. Zafar was tried before a military commission, convicted of treason, and exiled to Rangoon (present-day Yangon, Myanmar/Burma). He died in Rangoon in November 1862 — the last Mughal Emperor, buried in an unmarked grave. He was a noted poet; some of his Urdu verses written during exile are very moving. Lord Canning (Clemency Canning) opposed mass executions of rebels, but Zafar's exile was non-negotiable.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Bahadur Shah Zafar's fate (exile to Rangoon; death 1862) is a standard outcome question for the 1857 revolt

questions.text: Mangal Pandey is often called the first martyr of 1857. Which regiment was he from, and at which cantonment did his act of defiance occur?
options: 3rd Cavalry; Meerut cantonment
34th Bengal Native Infantry; Barrackpore cantonment
1st Bengal Fusiliers; Lucknow cantonment
19th Native Infantry; Ambala cantonment
questions.explanation: Mangal Pandey was a sepoy of the 34th Bengal Native Infantry (BNI) at Barrackpore cantonment (near Calcutta), not at Meerut. On 29 March 1857 — six weeks before the Meerut mutiny — he attacked and wounded British adjutant Lieutenant Baugh and Sergeant-Major Hewson when they came to arrest him. He called on his fellow soldiers to revolt; no one joined him. He was arrested, court-martialled, and hanged on 8 April 1857. His regiment (34th BNI) was disbanded for not arresting him earlier. The Meerut mutiny on 10 May 1857 is where the revolt formally began as a collective action. Mangal Pandey's individual act preceded it by six weeks at a different location (Barrackpore, not Meerut).
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because students consistently confuse Mangal Pandey's location (Barrackpore) with the Meerut mutiny; the regiment (34th BNI) and cantonment (Barrackpore) are specific detail questions in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Why did the revolt find strong support in Awadh (Lucknow) specifically, more than almost anywhere else?
options: Awadh had the highest concentration of Christian missionaries whose activities had enraged the population
The Bengal Army was predominantly recruited from Awadh; Awadh had been recently annexed (1856) with the Nawab Wajid Ali Shah exiled; sepoys' own families had been displaced and dispossessed
Awadh was the site where the greased cartridges were first distributed, so resentment was concentrated there
Awadh had the highest percentage of Brahmin zamindars who had lost land under Permanent Settlement
questions.explanation: The Awadh connection is key: the Bengal Army (which mutinied) drew a very large proportion of its recruits from Awadh (present-day eastern UP). When Dalhousie annexed Awadh in 1856, citing 'misgovernance,' the Nawab Wajid Ali Shah (a popular and culturally beloved ruler) was exiled to Calcutta. This hit the Bengal Army soldiers personally — their homes, families, and social networks were in Awadh; many had been loyal to the Nawab and had connections to the court. The annexation dispossessed not just the royal family but hundreds of nobles, court employees, musicians, poets, and their families. When these same soldiers were asked to bite the greased cartridges, they were already furious about Awadh. The Lucknow Residency siege and Begum Hazrat Mahal's leadership reflected how deep and personal this resentment was.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the structural reason for Awadh's centrality (Bengal Army = Awadh recruits + 1856 annexation) is an analytical cause-effect question in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Tatya Tope was the most capable military commander of the 1857 revolt and continued guerrilla warfare until 1859. How was he finally captured?
options: He was captured in battle by General Rose's forces near Gwalior
He surrendered to the British at Nagpur after being promised amnesty
He was betrayed by his friend Man Singh (Raja of Narwar) and handed over to the British
He was captured while trying to cross into Nepal to join Nana Sahib
questions.explanation: Tatya Tope (real name Ramchandra Pandurang) evaded British forces for nearly two years after the main revolt was suppressed — moving through Rajputana and central India conducting guerrilla warfare. He was finally betrayed by Man Singh, the Raja of Narwar, who had been sheltering him. Man Singh handed Tatya Tope over to the British, possibly in exchange for restoration of his estate and title. Tatya Tope was captured in the Paron forest in April 1859, tried by a military commission, and executed (hanged) on 18 April 1859 at Shivpuri. His betrayal by a trusted ally — and Nana Sahib's disappearance into Nepal without being captured — meant the revolt ended not with a final battle but with quiet endings.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Tatya Tope's ultimate fate (betrayed by Man Singh; executed 1859) is a specific outcome question that distinguishes him from Nana Sahib (disappeared) in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: The Lucknow Residency siege is a famous episode of 1857. Who finally relieved the besieged garrison, and in which month?
options: General Havelock finally relieved Lucknow in September 1857
General Havelock reached Lucknow in September 1857 but could not fully relieve it; Sir Colin Campbell finally relieved and evacuated the Residency in November 1857
Sir Colin Campbell relieved Lucknow in July 1857 in a single decisive action
The Residency garrison held out until March 1858 when Canning personally negotiated their release
questions.explanation: The Lucknow Residency siege has a two-stage relief — a common exam trap. The Residency was besieged from June 1857. General Henry Havelock (and General Outram) fought their way in to Lucknow in September 1857 — but could not fully relieve the garrison; they themselves became trapped inside with the original defenders. Sir Colin Campbell (Commander-in-Chief of India) finally relieved and safely evacuated the entire Residency garrison in November 1857, breaking through rebel lines with a powerful force. So: Havelock reached it in September; Campbell actually relieved it in November. Both names appear in exam options — the distinction is critical. The Residency ruins in Lucknow are still maintained as a historical monument.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the two-stage Lucknow relief (Havelock September / Campbell November) is a detail that surprises students who only know one name

questions.text: Kunwar Singh of Bihar is a celebrated figure of 1857 but is less commonly taught than Lakshmibai or Begum Hazrat Mahal. What is most remarkable about his participation?
options: He was the only Muslim leader of the revolt — remarkable because the revolt is often seen as Hindu-dominated

He was approximately 80 years old when he led the revolt, defeated multiple British columns across Bihar and UP, and died from wounds in April 1858 after reportedly cutting off his own wounded arm and offering it to the Ganga
He was a British-educated zamindar who turned against the British after reading V.D. Savarkar's writings
He commanded the largest rebel army — over 100,000 soldiers — making him the most powerful rebel military leader
questions.explanation: Kunwar Singh (c. 1777-1858) of Jagdispur, Arrah district in Bihar was remarkable for his age and military effectiveness. When the revolt broke out in 1857, he was approximately 80 years old — and yet he led a successful military campaign across Bihar and eastern UP, defeating British columns several times. He crossed the Ganga at Shivpuri (near Arrah) in April 1858 under British fire; a bullet hit his left arm. According to tradition, he cut off his own wounded arm with his sword and offered it to the Ganga — then crossed successfully. He died of his wounds on 26 April 1858, just days after reaching his home district. The story of the old zamindar fighting the empire in his 80s has made him a beloved figure in Bihar's memory of 1857.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Kunwar Singh is a Bihar-specific figure often skipped in national narratives; his age (~80) and the arm-cutting episode are specific details that appear in regional-focus and detail questions

questions.text: The Government of India Act 1858 ended Company rule. Which of the following was a direct institutional consequence?
options: The Indian National Congress was immediately established to provide Indian representation
The Secretary of State for India (a British Cabinet minister, with a Council of India) replaced the Board of Control and Court of Directors; the Governor-General became Viceroy
The East India Company was allowed to continue trading but lost political authority
India was formally declared a republic with Indian governors in each province
questions.explanation: The Government of India Act 1858 made specific institutional changes: (1) Abolished the East India Company's political role entirely; (2) Created a Secretary of State for India — a full Cabinet minister in London, responsible to Parliament, replacing the old Board of Control; (3) The Secretary of State was advised by a Council of India (15 members, mostly retired India hands) — they had to be consulted on major decisions; (4) The Governor-General's title changed to Viceroy (representing the Queen/King directly); (5) The Viceroy was advised by an Executive Council. Lord Canning became the first Viceroy. The Act also transferred the Company's armies (Bengal, Madras, Bombay armies) to the Crown. The INC was not founded until 1885 — 27 years later.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students know 'Company ended, Crown took over' but rarely know the specific institutional replacement (Secretary of State + Council of India replacing Board of Control + Court of Directors)

questions.text:
Match the leader of the 1857 revolt with their centre of activity:
1. Rani Lakshmibai    A. Kanpur
2. Begum Hazrat Mahal B. Jhansi
3. Nana Sahib          C. Lucknow
4. Kunwar Singh        D. Arrah (Bihar)
questions.explanation: Correct matches: (1) Rani Lakshmibai → B. Jhansi (her state, annexed under Doctrine of Lapse); (2) Begum Hazrat Mahal → C. Lucknow (wife of deposed Nawab Wajid Ali Shah; led Lucknow's resistance); (3) Nana Sahib → A. Kanpur (adopted son of Peshwa Baji Rao II; pension denied; led Kanpur revolt); (4) Kunwar Singh → D. Arrah, Bihar (ageing zamindar from Jagdispur). This leader-to-centre matching is the single most tested format for the 1857 revolt in SSC CGL. Note: Tatya Tope was Nana Sahib's military commander at Kanpur, not an independent centre leader. Maulvi Ahmadullah Shah and Khan Bahadur Khan (Bareilly) sometimes appear as additional options.
questions.source: Leader-centre matching for 1857 is the most frequently tested question format; appears in virtually every SSC CGL paper covering the revolt

questions.text: Which of the following was the IMMEDIATE cause of the Revolt of 1857?
options: The annexation of Awadh by Lord Dalhousie in 1856
The General Service Enlistment Act of 1856 requiring overseas service
The rumour that the new Enfield rifle cartridges were greased with pig and cow fat, requiring soldiers to bite them
The Doctrine of Lapse and annexation of Jhansi in 1853
questions.explanation: The IMMEDIATE cause of the 1857 revolt was the introduction of the new Enfield Rifle (Pattern 1853) and the rumour that its cartridges were greased with pig fat (offensive to Muslims) and cow fat (offensive to Hindus). Soldiers had to bite off the top of the paper cartridge before loading — direct oral contact with the offensive substance. This ignited pre-existing resentments. The other options are underlying/background causes: the Doctrine of Lapse (Jhansi 1853) and annexation of Awadh (1856) created political resentment; the General Service Enlistment Act (1856) created specific military grievances about overseas service. All are important, but 'immediate' or 'immediate trigger' = greased cartridges.
questions.source: The immediate cause of 1857 (greased cartridges / Enfield rifle) vs. underlying causes is one of the most frequently tested cause-classification questions in SSC CGL Modern History

questions.text: Which of the following areas did NOT actively participate in the 1857 revolt, and why?
options: Lucknow — because the Nawab had signed a treaty of peace with the British
Punjab — because Sikhs, recently conquered by the British, resented Mughal authority more than British rule and actively helped the British suppress Delhi
Bihar — because the zamindars of Bihar were loyal British allies
Rajputana — because the princes had all signed the Subsidiary Alliance in 1798
questions.explanation: Punjab is the most important example of an area that did not join the revolt and actively helped suppress it. The Sikhs had been conquered by the British only in 1849 (Second Anglo-Sikh War), but their resentment of Mughal authority ran deeper — the Mughals had persecuted Sikh Gurus (Guru Arjan Dev Ji and Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji were executed by Mughals). When Delhi's rebels proclaimed a Mughal Emperor, Sikhs saw this as a Mughal restoration, not a fight for their freedom. Sikh soldiers and Punjab Irregular Forces fought alongside the British in the siege of Delhi. Madras and Bombay armies also stayed largely loyal — different recruitment regions, different grievances. Lucknow was one of the centres of active revolt (not peaceful — option A is wrong). Bihar DID participate (Kunwar Singh, option C is wrong).
questions.source: Why the revolt didn't spread to Punjab (Sikh resentment of Mughals) is a frequently tested analytical question about the revolt's limitations
